chunk.text: 1 © 2012: IntierraRMG (UK) Ltd Email: reports@intierra.com Monthly Copper Briefing Report June 2012 LME Cash falls by over $500/t Cash to 3-month backwarda on collapsing Output numbers below expecta on Copper use numbers disappoint even more Projects re-assessed on lower price outlook Not only has the LME Cash price fallen by more than $500/t to range around $7,700/t this month, the mood of the market had changed drama!cally. With the apparent !ghtness in physical copper in April, resul!ng from the concentra!on of available stock in China and a strong holding of LME stock, there had been good reason for copper prices to remain high. The Cash to 3- month backwarda!on was symptoma!c of the market tone. In May, however, the backwarda!on has all but dissipated. As stocks relocate out of China, there is no longer a dominant stock holder on the LME and shorts, that had been scared oﬀ by the backwarda!on, begin to re-enter the scene, the copper market of today looks very diﬀerent from a month ago. Despite the entry of shorts, open interest on the LME has fallen to much lower levels. This results from long liquida!on, reﬂec!ng a growing consensus that lower prices are here to stay.

list. For copper at least, some of the projects that had been seen as near-certain will fall by the wayside as a result of this changing world view. is comfortable, there is not very much at all in much of the rest of the world, especially Europe, where LME inventory has fallen to less than 20 kt. Going forward, though, a lack of dynamicism in China and a feed-through of the Eurozone crisis to the real economy gives us less hope that consump!on will pick up much in coming months, Copper use is almost certain to struggle to keep up with growing copper output. The struggle would already be greater had not copper output con!nued to come in below expecta!on.. A wide acceptance of a more nega!ve forward view is really the over-riding theme right now for copper, as for other metals. The conﬁdent asser!on un!l recently by mining companies that China will con!nue to deliver huge annual increments to metals demand has been replaced by a much more cau!ous stance. As a result, companies such as BHP and Rio Tinto are looking again at their (increasingly expensive) project list. For copper at least, some of the projects that had been seen as near-certain will fall by the wayside as a result of this changing world view.

erest on the LME has fallen to much lower levels. This results from long liquida!on, reﬂec!ng a growing consensus that lower prices are here to stay. The fall in prices occurred earlier than we had expected. We had been of the view that engineered !ghtness in copper would merge seamlessly into real !ghtness as consump!on picked up to absorb surplus copper. In terms of developments in the demand / supply balance and overall liquid stock change, we weren’t so far wrong. What we hadn’t calculated on was the speed at which stocks would start to relocate out of China (from bonded warehouses and from smelters), nor the early collapse of the dominant stock holder - backwarda!on structure on the LME. Looking at the underlying market fundamentals alone, we s!ll have some reason to be op!mis!c. In May, exchange stocks have fallen by 80 kt, and Chinese merchant bonded stocks by at least this amount to reach around 550kt. Also, excess stocks at Chinese smelters have fallen. Thus, while there is s!ll more liquid stock in China than is comfortable, there is not very much at all in much of the rest of the world, especially Europe, where LME inventory has fallen to less than 20 kt.

3 © 2012: IntierraRMG (UK) Ltd Email: reports@intierra.com Copper Prices Track Lower From early May, when LME Cash had comfortably regained a price tag of around $8,500/t, prices started to fall. The slide was quite rapid, over the ﬁrst two weeks of the month taking LME Cash down to a new level in the region of $7,700/t., where it is now placed. In the past week, even quite bullish news including the promise of an RMB 2 billion s!mulus package in China and a temporary stoppage at Collahuasi, has failed to liM prices by that much. Lower prices in part reﬂect a strengthening US Dollar, but only in part. As for forward prices, the LME Cash to three-month backwarda!on that has been such a feature of the copper market for more than two months has started to collapse. Whereas the spread was over $100/t at the start of May, it is now around $30/t and falling. With lower London prices, the gap in pricing with Shanghai has widened. Domes!c Chinese SHFE one- month prices are now around 15% higher than LME Cash, compared to just over 10% at the start of May.

Shanghai has widened. Domes!c Chinese SHFE one- month prices are now around 15% higher than LME Cash, compared to just over 10% at the start of May. Although this is not suﬃcient to allow a pure arbitrage trade, including duty and related costs, the eﬀec!ve gap is now less than $100/t rather than over $500/t. Visible Stocks Fall and Relocate While prices have been falling, the physical market has started to look !ghter. In May so far, total exchange stocks have fallen by 80 kt. The biggest reduc!on, of 48 kt, has been on the SHFE. Comex also has seen a drop while, aMer a 20 kt fall in the ﬁrst two weeks of the month, total LME stocks have been more-or-less sta!c at around 225 kt. The net movement of stock on the LME has been quite small, but the loca!on of stock has changed quite drama!cally. The United States has seen a sharp drawdown of around 25 kt. More important, a slightly smaller shiM out of Europe has leM the region’s LME stock at less than 20 kt, a very low level indeed. In contrast, Asian stocks in Korea and Singapore have seen a big increase. The rise in Asian LME stock reﬂects movement out of China.

w level indeed. In contrast, Asian stocks in Korea and Singapore have seen a big increase. The rise in Asian LME stock reﬂects movement out of China. With a nearby backwarda!on making holding metal less aKrac!ve and rela!vely low SHFE prices discouraging domes!c deliveries, Chinese merchant bonded copper has been released on to the LME. In both March and April, China exported over 25 kt of reﬁned copper. This is unusual, as China rarely exports much cathode. It seems, however, that the export volume from China is rising, and more from the smelters than the bonded warehouses. 6 © Copyright: Photocopying and Electronic Forwarding Prohibited Email: reports@intierra.com Purchase Team and Xiangguang Copper announced their inten!on to export copper over the next two months. Mid-month, it was reported that 100 kt in export contracts had been signed, of which 80 kt came from four large smelters. The logic behind the Chinese smelter move is clear. Not only do they have excess stock which they need to unload, also, they have been hurt by the backwarda!on as their purchases of concentrate are marked against current prices, their sales of cathode against future prices. By moving copper out of China, the controlling interest on LME stock that underlies the backwarda!on should be less easy to maintain. With excess cathode moving out of China, the Chinese physical market is looking brighter. Stocks are moving from SHFE warehouses to domes!c consumers and spot premia are edging upwards, though s!ll below $50/t on imported copper. Outside Asia, upward pressure on spot premia remains, with low liquid stocks behind this.

edging upwards, though s!ll below $50/t on imported copper. Outside Asia, upward pressure on spot premia remains, with low liquid stocks behind this. Spot TC/RCs Remain Low As Chinese smelters are looking to reduce stocks of reﬁned copper, not surprisingly, they has liKle appe!te for new concentrates. In April, despite a 3.7% drop in mine output compared to March, concentrates imports were down by 19%. This means minimal spot TC/RC business in China. Chinese smelters are looking for spot terms of $50/dmt and 5 c/lb, but there appear to be no takers at this level. In contrast, smelters in India and Korea are reported to be short of material. 7 © 2012: IntierraRMG (UK) Ltd Email: reports@intierra.com Macroeconomic Trends Consump<on Trends The odds that Greece exits the Euro have been drama!cally increased in the past weeks as the front- running poli!cal par!es were unable, or unwilling, to form a coali!on government aMer the divisive result of May elec!ons. New elec!ons are planned for June 17th, but hopes for a las!ng Greek solu!on are diminishing. Now, European oﬃcials quite openly acknowledge they are working on a con!ngency plan for a “Grexit”; Chris!ne Lagarde says the IMF is considering this too. It seems that the ques!on is not anymore if, but when, and how. Especially the “how”, as this has deep implica!ons for what happens next in the other troubled countries of southern Europe. With US$1.2 trillion in Spanish, Portuguese, Italian, and Irish debt, Europe’s lenders s!ll face deposit ﬂight risk and rising defaults elsewhere. Many fear a domino eﬀect following a Greek exit, with a call on bail out funds that simply cannot, or will not, be met.

nd rising defaults elsewhere. Many fear a domino eﬀect following a Greek exit, with a call on bail out funds that simply cannot, or will not, be met. As for China, recent economic data shows further slowdown, especially in middle-market manufacturing, indica!ng that problems in China’s export industries may be turning structural. Also, latest trade balance ﬁgures show slowing imports, when normal seasonal paKerns suggest an increase. Global IP rose by an es!mated 0.2% on a month-on- month basis in March aMer rising by 0.3% in February. OECD produc!on fell by 0.2% while non-OECD output was up by 0.7%. While Chinese IP growth was down, ﬂash PMI for May suggest that there may be more to come. The index fell to 48.7 in May from a ﬁnal reading of 49.3 in April, stoking fears that the Chinese economy could slow sharply. The Chinese government is looking for ways to bring forward the start up of infrastructure projects that had been originally planned to commence in 2013. This is to counter weak economic growth. That, in combina!on with the new subsidies for energy eﬃcient consumer goods announced in May, is expected to have posi!ve impact on the demand for copper products.

li!es and for building wire to remain stable in the next months, with beKer prospects in submarine, oﬀshore oil and gas and renewable energy markets. Meanwhile, copper/alloy mill products company KME, which suﬀered a 15% output decline for its semi-ﬁnished, copper alloy and special products in the ﬁrst quarter, predicts that the weak demand from the construc!on industry will con!nue. Certainly, this appears to be the case over much of Europe. As for the automo!ve sector, German harness producer Leoni, sees substan!al growth, especially in the United States and the emerging geographies, oﬀseWng a weaker market in Europe. -15% -10% -5% 0% 5% 10% 15% 0 1000 2000 3000 4000 5000 6000 Q1 Q3 Q1 Q3 Q1 Q3 Q1 Q3 Q1 Q3 Q1 Q3 2008 2009 2010 2011 2012 2013 Y-o-Y Change Refined Copper Use Refined Copper Use Trend Kt Y-o-Y Change -40% -30% -20% -10% 0% 10% 20% 30% 2008 2009 2010 2011 2012 Germany United States Japan China IP Development in Selected Countries Y-o-Y Change

a!on with the new subsidies for energy eﬃcient consumer goods announced in May, is expected to have posi!ve impact on the demand for copper products. However, there is liKle sign yet in the order books that the restraint in copper use will improve much in coming months. Demand for copper tubes remains slow as sales of air condi!oners is s!ll down on last year, although not by as much as the 8% year-on-year drop in January-February. Power cable ﬁgures are more posi!ve, with output up by 11% year-on-year in Q1 2012. This market is expected to strengthen as a result of increased spending by the power u!li!es. As the major fabricators publish their Q1 ﬁnancials, the consump!on trends so far and expecta!ons for the remainder of this year become clearer. Cable producer Prysmian expects global demand for medium voltage cables for u!li!es and for building wire to remain stable in the next months, with beKer prospects in submarine, oﬀshore oil and gas and renewable energy markets.

9 © 2012: IntierraRMG (UK) Ltd Email: reports@intierra.com Appe<te for Mine Investment Lower The mining industry is entering into a new era as the “supercycle” in commodity prices, driven in part by China’s rapid infrastructure build out, appears to be coming to a close. BHP Billiton and Rio Tinto have acknowledged the sea change, and have announced plans to re-evaluate their development porYolio. Casual!es for copper were almost immediate. BHP quit a small joint venture to deﬁne the Al!a copper-silver prospect in Queensland, Australia just a day aMer the company's leadership warned it would cut back on spending. On a much larger scale, BHP is likely to revise its hugely ambi!ous US$30 billion expansion of Olympic Dam. Whether or not the 750 ktpy project is the next in line to be postponed, delayed, downsized or even cancelled will be decided in June. But, the point is, the cancella!on of such large projects has now entered into the realm of the possible. The prospect of lower than expected demand growth and falling prices bring into focus a number of issues that, un!l now, haven’t really aﬀected future investment plans that much. Now these issues really maKer.

g prices bring into focus a number of issues that, un!l now, haven’t really aﬀected future investment plans that much. Now these issues really maKer. One is the sharp re-evalua!on of capital costs for many planned projects. Rio Tinto now es!mates that Oyu Tolgoi in Mongolia will cost US$13.2 billion instead of the US$9.55 billion es!mated earlier. Project ﬁnancing, too, has become more of a headache. Having decided to go alone on the Cobre Panama project, Inmet is faced with having to ﬁnance US$6.2 billion development, up from the previously es!mated US$4.3 billion. To do this, it plans to raise US$1 billion with a senior unsecured note oﬀering and is considering a further US$1 billion in a precious metals stream ﬁnancing deal. Not only do even the larger companies have to be more innova!ve in ﬁnancing, the process is also becoming less certain. CNMC, currently targe!ng to raise capital through the sale of shares on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange to ﬁnance Zambian developments, has adjusted its ambi!ons, expec!ng to raise US$313 million rather than the originally planned US$500 million.

oo will turn away from new investment remains to be seen. change to ﬁnance Zambian developments, has adjusted its ambi!ons, expec!ng to raise US$313 million rather than the originally planned US$500 million. Add to this par!al withdrawal from projects such as Tia Maria and Congo in Peru where environmental objec!ons hold huge risks, uncertain permiWng by government in countries such as Indonesia and the Philippines and increased government take in taxes, and it seems that developments that not so long ago were certain are no longer so. Despite the changed tone, however, some companies, especially those scrambling to secure feed for their processing plant, s!ll seem keen to invest in mining. This applies in par!cular to the Japanese and Chinese smelters. Whether they too will turn away from new investment remains to be seen.

© Copyright: Photocopying and Electronic Forwarding Prohibited Email: reports@intierra.com Mining News Argen<na: Xstrata has stated that it is monitoring poli!cal risk in Argen!na aMer the na!onalisa!on of 51% of YPF SA, the country’s largest oil company. Also, a recent requirement by government for miners to present plans to replace imports with goods made domes!cally concerns the company. Xstrata is currently moving forward with two copper projects, the US$4 billion El Pachon and US$1.5 billion Agua Rica, both in feasibility study. Xstrata is opera!ng the Alumbrera copper mine in Argen!na. Mining.com, May 24th 2012. Australia: Gold-copper miner Ivanhoe Australia Ltd. has received interest from up to eight bidders for stakes in four of its projects. The proceeds from the stake sale are key for Ivanhoe Australia to develop its Osborne copper- gold mine, the Merlin molybdenum-rhenium project, the Mount Dore copper cathode project and the Mount EllioI copper-gold project. Second-round bids are due late in May, with preliminary interest seen from Anglo American, OZ Minerals, Vedanta Group and Indian conglomerate Aditya Birla Group for the copper and gold deposits.

reliminary interest seen from Anglo American, OZ Minerals, Vedanta Group and Indian conglomerate Aditya Birla Group for the copper and gold deposits. The company aims to complete a deal or deals by mid-2012. Mineweb, May 15th 2012. Brazil: Vale has completed construc!on of the ﬁrst phase of the Salobo mine in Carajas. This ﬁrst phase of the project should produce around 100 ktpy of copper in concentrate. If built, the mine's Phase II will double produc!on capacity. Mining.com, April 30th 2012. The Federal Supreme Court has suspended the payment of around R$24 billion (US$12 billion) in back taxes by Vale un!l the court has reviewed the case in a plenary session. The miner is ﬁgh!ng four ac!ons by the Brazilian Federal Revenue Service, which claims that Vale has avoided paying levies on proﬁts obtained at foreign subsidiaries. The company indicates that it has already paid money to foreign governments and that taxing the company again is an unfair burden. The tax claims, which date back from the early 2000s, amount to more than half Vale's US$22.9 billion proﬁt in 2011 and nearly all of its 2010 proﬁt.

The tax claims, which date back from the early 2000s, amount to more than half Vale's US$22.9 billion proﬁt in 2011 and nearly all of its 2010 proﬁt. If the decision holds up in the courts, it could open the door for cases against other Brazilian-based companies that have tax liabili!es on foreign earnings. Thomson Reuters, May 10th 2012. Chile: In April, San!ago's 14th Civil Court rejected a bid by Codelco to force 49% of dividend payments rela!ng to Anglo American’s disputed Anglo Sur assets to be retained in escrow. The miners clashed in October 2011 when Anglo sold 24.5% of those proper!es to Mitsubishi before Codelco’s window to exercise an op!on to buy up a 49% stake in the assets expired for this year in January. In May. Anglo American and Codelco requested the suspension of legal proceedings at the Court un!l June 22nd while they explore a possible resolu!on. The companies held two months of private talks in December and January, but failed to come to an agreement at that !me. Prior to a recent agreement to suspend the ac!on, Codelco es!mated that the legal baKle could drag on for up to four years. Thomson Reuters April 26th 2012, Mineweb May 23rd 2012.

Indonesia: The Indonesian government has started to implement a 20% export duty on 21 metal ore and concentrates products, including copper, under new regula!ons applied from May 6th.

nt has started to implement a 20% export duty on 21 metal ore and concentrates products, including copper, under new regula!ons applied from May 6th. The government aims to boost investment in domes!c ore processing and to liM exports of higher value ﬁnished metals. Energy and Minerals Minister Jero Wacik said that the regula!on "will not allow anyone to export raw material, unless they submiKed a roadmap to build a smelter". Up to now, the ministry has received 82 proposals for smel!ng projects out of the 400 mining ﬁrms working in Indonesia. In addi!on, the government intends to implement export quotas between June and August, the amount allowed for each ﬁrm being based on 2009 or 2010 export performance. The new rules do not apply to companies such as Freeport-McMoRan, Vale Indonesia and Newmont Corp., which hold decades-long Contracts of Work (COW), although the government has indicated that it wishes to renego!ate even these agreements. Thomson Reuters, May 4th and May 16th 2012.
